clue: Statue of Liberty
fun_fact: She was a gift from France, but New Yorkers had to fundraise to build her pedestal.

clue: Empire State Building
fun_fact: It was built in just 410 days during the Great Depression.

clue: Brooklyn Bridge
fun_fact: Its chief engineer died from an injury sustained during its construction. His wife oversaw the rest of the build.

clue: Grand Central Terminal
fun_fact: The ceiling's famous constellation mural is painted backwards — a medieval manuscript error copied faithfully.

clue: Yankee Stadium
fun_fact: The current stadium opened in 2009, directly across the street from the original.

clue: The High Line
fun_fact: It was saved from demolition by two neighbors who fought for it for nearly a decade.

clue: Coney Island Cyclone
fun_fact: Charles Lindbergh rode it and said it was scarier than flying the Atlantic.

clue: Unisphere
fun_fact: It was built for the 1964 World's Fair and weighs 700,000 pounds.

clue: Snug Harbor Cultural Center
fun_fact: Its Greek Revival buildings are among the finest examples of the style in the United States.

clue: Flatiron Building
fun_fact: The wind tunnel it creates on 23rd Street was so notorious that police coined the phrase '23 skidoo' to shoo away men watching women's skirts blow up.

clue: The corner where the Dutch first established a marketplace in New Amsterdam
fun_fact: This area was the commercial heart of Manhattan before the city expanded northward.

clue: The Washington Heights corner where Malcolm X was assassinated in 1965
fun_fact: The Audubon Ballroom where he was shot has since been converted into a biomedical research center, with a small memorial preserved in the lobby.

clue: The epicenter of Bedford-Stuyvesant, named after a Lenape chief
fun_fact: Bed-Stuy takes its name from two colonial-era towns that merged: Bedford and Stuyvesant Heights.

clue: Once nicknamed the Boulevard of Death for its deadly pedestrian crossings
fun_fact: After a years-long safety campaign, Queens Blvd was redesigned with protected lanes and pedestrian islands, and fatalities dropped dramatically.

clue: The corner that puts you right at the gates of Yankee Stadium
fun_fact: The elevated 4 train that runs along Jerome Ave has carried Yankees fans to games since 1918.

clue: Where SoHo ends, Chinatown begins, and Tribeca waves from across the street
fun_fact: Canal Street follows the path of an actual canal that was filled in 1820 to create the road.

clue: The end of the line — literally — for the D, F, N, and Q trains
fun_fact: The Stillwell Avenue station is the largest above-ground subway terminal in the world.

clue: The Staten Island block where hip-hop history was quietly being written
fun_fact: Several Wu-Tang Clan members grew up in the Park Hill Apartments nearby, which they called Killa Hill.

clue: Where Bushwick begins and the J/M/Z rumbles overhead
fun_fact: The elevated J/M/Z train above Broadway here was part of the inspiration for the 'el' scenes in countless NYC films.

clue: The grandest intersection on the Bronx's answer to the Champs-Élysées
fun_fact: The Grand Concourse was modeled after Paris's grand boulevards and was once lined with Art Deco apartment buildings.

clue: Where Meg Ryan proved a point about authenticity
fun_fact: Table 25 still has a sign above it marking the exact spot from When Harry Met Sally.

clue: The diner exterior that a generation knows as Monk's Cafe
fun_fact: It also inspired the Suzanne Vega song 'Tom's Diner,' recorded while she was a student at nearby Barnard College.

clue: The Tribeca firehouse that became headquarters for New York's most unconventional pest control team
fun_fact: It's a fully functioning firehouse. The Ghostbusters sign outside is a permanent tribute.

clue: The waterfront esplanade where Moonstruck, Annie Hall, and countless other films captured the Manhattan skyline
fun_fact: Woody Allen filmed the opening sequence of Manhattan here at 4am to capture the sunrise over the skyline.

clue: The Central Park pond where Stuart Little raced his sailboat
fun_fact: Model boat racing has been a tradition here since the 1930s. Rentable remote-control boats are still available on weekends.

clue: The borough that one rap collective put on the map by calling it Shaolin
fun_fact: Wu-Tang Clan named Staten Island 'Shaolin' after the Shaolin Temple in kung fu films, a nod to their martial arts obsession.

clue: Tony Manero strutted across this bridge in the opening scene of Saturday Night Fever
answer_label: 86th St & New Utrecht Ave, Bay Ridge
fun_fact: The film put Bay Ridge on the map. The neighborhood still holds an annual Saturday Night Fever tribute walk.

clue: The fictional MIB headquarters is hidden beneath this real Queens landmark
fun_fact: The Queens Museum houses a 9,335 square foot scale model of every building in NYC, called the Panorama of the City of New York.

clue: Where Alex the Lion dreamed of something wilder than New York
fun_fact: The Bronx Zoo is one of the largest metropolitan zoos in the world, covering 265 acres.

clue: Travis Bickle's cab circled this roundabout obsessively in Scorsese's 1976 masterpiece
fun_fact: Columbus Circle is the official geographic center from which all NYC distances are measured.
